issues.description: FR-6b says disk usage is obtained via 'du -sh /cache/*' executed on 'the most recent completed or running agent pod.' However, you cannot kubectl exec into a completed pod — the container process has exited. Only running pods accept exec. The spec conflates 'completed' (exists in API but container stopped) with 'available for exec' (container running). In practice, agent pods complete quickly and the window for a running pod is small, so disk usage will frequently be 'unavailable' unless the implementation specifically targets only running pods or uses a different mechanism (e.g., spawning an ephemeral debug container via kubectl debug, or reading PV data from the node).
issues.suggestion: Clarify that disk inspection is only possible on running pods (not completed ones). Either (a) restrict the language to 'most recent running agent pod' and accept that usage is often unavailable, or (b) specify an alternative mechanism for inspecting completed pod PVs (e.g., Kubernetes ephemeral debug containers or a lightweight inspection pod). Note this only affects FR-6 which is a separable milestone.

issues.description: FR-3e says cache config is repo-level only and 'cluster and engineer configs do not contribute cache settings.' The existing config system in merged.rs implements three-layer merging (cluster → repo → engineer) for all config sections. The spec does not specify what happens if an operator accidentally places a [cache] section in their cluster or engineer config. Should it be silently ignored? Should it produce a warning? Should deserialization fail? Without guidance, an implementer might let serde deserialize it from all layers and accidentally merge it.
issues.suggestion: Add a sentence specifying the behavior when [cache] appears in cluster or engineer config: e.g., 'If [cache] appears in cluster or engineer configs, it is silently ignored — the repo-level [cache] is the sole source.' Alternatively, note that the CacheConfig field should only be read from the repo-layer config struct, not from the merged config.

issues.description: FR-3b defines three distinct behaviors based on the presence/absence of [cache] and [cache.env]: (1) [cache] absent → sccache defaults, (2) [cache] present but [cache.env] absent/empty → no env vars, (3) [cache] present with [cache.env] entries → use them. Distinguishing case 1 from case 2 with serde requires using Option<CacheConfig> rather than the #[serde(default)] pattern used by every other config section in mod.rs. The spec's behavioral requirements are clear, but the implementation divergence from the established config pattern is non-obvious and could trip up an implementer who follows the existing pattern.
issues.suggestion: Add an implementation note: 'The cache field on NautiloopConfig must use Option<CacheConfig> (not #[serde(default)]) so that the absent-section case (None → inject sccache defaults) is distinguishable from the present-but-empty case (Some with empty env → no cache env vars).'

issues.description: FR-6b introduces a GET /dashboard/cache endpoint. The existing API uses flat route prefixes (/start, /status, /logs/{id}, etc.) with no /dashboard/* namespace. This is a new URL pattern. Not wrong, but diverges from the current API structure.
issues.suggestion: Consider whether /dashboard/cache should be /cache instead, matching the flat route pattern. Alternatively, note in the spec that this intentionally introduces a /dashboard namespace for future read-only introspection endpoints.

issues.description: Files Likely Touched lists 'control-plane/src/config/repo.rs + merged.rs' for the CacheConfig struct, but the NautiloopConfig struct (where the cache field must be added) lives in control-plane/src/config/mod.rs, not repo.rs. repo.rs contains RepoConfig (repo metadata like name, org, url) which is a different structure.
issues.suggestion: Update 'Files Likely Touched' to reference control-plane/src/config/mod.rs for the CacheConfig struct addition to NautiloopConfig.

issues.description: FR-6a's example output shows 'Disk usage: 2.1 GiB / 50 GiB (4%)' but there is no specification of how the total PVC size is obtained at runtime. The control plane knows the requested size from terraform/manifests, but actual PV capacity may differ. Is it read from the PVC spec, the PV status, or the config?
issues.suggestion: Clarify that PVC capacity is read from the PVC's status.capacity field via the Kubernetes API, or from the nemo.toml/terraform config value.
